critic_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT:

The paper uses concordance index (C=0.79) as the primary evidence for deployment of a survival model in a capacity-constrained oncology referral context. This is a metric mismatch.

The C-index measures global pairwise rank accuracy across all patient pairs. The referral program operates at a single threshold: the top 12% of patients receive immediate slots. What matters at this operating point is not global rank accuracy but top-slice PPV and sensitivity, and net benefit at the budget constraint. These are different quantities. It is possible — not just hypothetical — for a model with C=0.79 to produce a top-12% list with no better composition than the current clinical heuristic, particularly when the 12th-percentile boundary is where model uncertainty concentrates.

The paper's gap in evaluation: (1) no PPV or sensitivity at 12% referral capacity; (2) no decision-curve analysis showing the model produces net benefit relative to the cost of false positives (nurse and social-work time); (3) no lead-time analysis — the 90-day prediction window does not establish that patients are identified early enough for program enrollment and benefit; (4) no comparison against the current heuristic.

The external validation is a genuine methodological strength — it shows the model generalizes. Routine EHR features are a deployment advantage. But neither property addresses the triage utility question at the referral budget.

Verdict: critique_wins.
